chunk_summary: Guidelines for selecting short versus standard antibiotic courses in acute septic arthritis, emphasizing patient-specific factors and clinical response.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for selecting short versus standard antibiotic courses in acute septic arthritis.
Identify clinical and laboratory markers that guide antibiotic duration decisions.
Recognize situations where longer antibiotic courses are mandatory.
Evaluate the role of imaging and follow-up in managing septic arthritis.
key_facts: Traditional treatment for uncomplicated septic arthritis is 2-3 weeks of antibiotics after drainage.
Shorter courses (10-14 days) are possible for selected children with rapid improvement and no complications.
Criteria for shorter courses include afebrile status by the first week, steady improvement in joint mobility, and a clear downward CRP trajectory.
Longer courses are required for MRSA, bacteremia, delayed drainage, neonatal disease, or hip involvement.
Families must ensure prompt follow-up if symptoms recur after transitioning to oral antibiotics.
reference_quote: Newer international guidance allows a shorter total course, sometimes as little as ten to fourteen days, in carefully selected children with primary bacterial arthritis who improve rapidly after prompt drainage.
